preamble.summary: Psalm 9 is a thanksgiving and lament psalm with an acrostic structure — each stanza begins with a successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet (though the pattern is incomplete; Psalms 9-10 together form the full acrostic). The psalmist praises God for judging the nations, rebuking the wicked, and defending the oppressed. He calls on God to arise and judge, declaring that the nations are mere mortals and the needy will not be forgotten forever.
preamble.remarkable: This psalm introduces the theological concept that is central to much of the Psalter: God as the refuge and stronghold of the oppressed. The phrase misgav la-dakh ('a stronghold for the crushed') in verse 10 establishes a pattern that runs through the entire collection — God's power is directed specifically toward those who have no other power. The acrostic structure (aleph through kaph in Psalm 9, then continuing lamed through tav in Psalm 10) suggests that the two psalms were originally a single composition, an A-to-Z declaration that God rules justly from beginning to end.
preamble.friction: The acrostic pattern in Psalm 9 is imperfect — some letters are missing or appear out of order, which may indicate textual corruption, deliberate artistic variation, or the limits of fitting theology into a rigid alphabetic framework. The superscription mentions almut labben, a phrase of uncertain meaning (possibly 'death of the son,' a melody name, or a corruption of alamot, 'maidens'). The psalm alternates between past-tense praise (God has judged) and present-tense lament (enemies persist), creating a tension between confidence in what God has done and urgency about what God must still do.
preamble.connections: Psalms 9 and 10 form a literary pair connected by acrostic structure and shared vocabulary. The 'pit they dug' image in verse 16 echoes Psalm 7:16. The cry qumah YHWH ('arise, O LORD') in verse 20 echoes Psalms 3:8 and 7:7. The declaration that God 'does not forget the cry of the afflicted' (v. 13) anticipates the theology of Psalms 10, 12, 22, and 34. The phrase 'the needy shall not always be forgotten' (v. 19) is the Psalter's most direct promise to the poor.

verses.text_hebrew: לַמְנַצֵּ֥חַ עַלְמ֥וּת לַבֵּ֗ן מִזְמ֥וֹר לְדָוִֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician upon Muthlabben, A Psalm of David.
verses.rendering:
For the director of music. According to 'The Death of the Son.'
A psalm of David.
translator_notes: Almut labben (or mut labben) is one of the most obscure superscription terms. It may mean 'death of the son' (mavvet la-ben), possibly indicating a melody named for a song about a son's death. Some scholars repoint it as al alamot labben ('according to maidens' voices, for the son'). Others treat it as a liturgical or musical term whose original meaning was lost before the Masoretes vocalized the text. We transliterate the traditional reading while acknowledging its uncertainty.

verses.text_hebrew: אוֹדֶ֣ה יְ֭הוָה בְּכׇל־לִבִּ֑י אֲ֝סַפְּרָ֗ה כׇּל־נִפְלְאוֹתֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: I will praise thee, O LORD, with my whole heart; I will shew forth all thy marvellous works.
verses.rendering:
I will give thanks to the LORD with all my heart;
I will recount all Your wonders.
verses.expanded_rendering: The acrostic begins with aleph. Odeh YHWH be-khol libbi ('I will give thanks to the LORD with my whole heart') — the totality of the inner person (kol libbi, 'all my heart') is committed to gratitude. This is not casual acknowledgment but whole-hearted thanksgiving. Asapperah kol nifle'otekha ('I will recount all Your wonders') — the verb saphar ('to count, to recount, to tell') means to narrate — to transform divine acts into story and testimony. Nifle'ot ('wonders, marvelous deeds') from pala ('to be extraordinary, to do the impossible') refers to acts that exceed natural expectation — God's interventions that break the ordinary course of events. The psalmist commits to leaving nothing out: kol ('all') appears twice, framing comprehensive gratitude.
translator_notes: The verb saphar ('to recount, to tell') is related to sefer ('book, document'). To recount God's wonders is to make them publicly known, to turn experience into testimony. The Psalter insists that praise is not private but communal — what God has done must be told.

verses.text_hebrew: אֶשְׂמְחָ֣ה וְאֶעֶלְצָ֣ה בָ֑ךְ אֲזַמְּרָ֖ה שִׁמְךָ֣ עֶלְיֽוֹן׃
verses.text_kjv: I will be glad and rejoice in thee: I will sing praise to thy name, O thou most High.
verses.rendering:
I will rejoice and exult in You;
I will sing praise to Your name, O Most High.
translator_notes: The bet stanza opens with three verbs of joy: esmechah ('I will rejoice'), e'eltsah ('I will exult'), azammerah ('I will sing praise'). The title Elyon ('Most High') from Psalm 7:18 returns, affirming God's supreme sovereignty as the foundation for joy. The joy is directed bekha ('in You') — not in circumstances but in God's person.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּשׁ֣וּב אוֹיְבַ֣י אָח֑וֹר יִכָּשְׁל֥וּ וְ֝יֹאבְד֗וּ מִפָּנֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: When mine enemies are turned back, they shall fall and perish at thy presence.
verses.rendering:
When my enemies turn back,
they stumble and perish before You.
verses.expanded_rendering: The reason for thanksgiving: be-shuv oy'vai achor ('when my enemies turn backward, retreat'). The enemies' retreat is not natural cowardice but the result of encountering God. Yikkashlu ('they stumble, they falter') — the verb kashal means to totter and fall, losing one's footing. Ve-yov'du mippanekha ('and they perish from before Your face') — the enemies are destroyed by God's panekha ('face, presence'). The divine face that the righteous seek (Psalm 27:8) is the same face that the wicked cannot endure. God's presence is simultaneously refuge for the faithful and destruction for the hostile.
translator_notes: The phrase mippanekha ('from before Your face, from Your presence') is theologically loaded. The 'face' of God in the Psalter represents direct encounter with the divine. For the righteous, it means blessing (Psalm 4:7); for the wicked, it means annihilation. The same presence produces opposite effects depending on the moral orientation of the one who encounters it.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־עָ֭שִׂיתָ מִשְׁפָּטִ֣י וְדִינִ֑י יָשַׁ֥בְתָּ לְ֝כִסֵּ֗א שׁוֹפֵ֥ט צֶֽדֶק׃
verses.text_kjv: For thou hast maintained my right and my cause; thou satest in the throne judging right.
verses.rendering:
For You have upheld my case and my cause;
You sit on the throne, judging with righteousness.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist testifies to God's judicial action in his personal case. Asita mishpati ve-dini ('You have executed my judgment and my cause') — mishpat and din are both legal terms: God has taken the psalmist's case, heard the evidence, and ruled in his favor. Yashavta le-khisse shophet tsedeq ('You have sat on the throne, a judge of righteousness') — God is pictured as a king-judge on an elevated seat of authority, and the quality of His judgment is tsedeq ('righteousness, equity, justice'). The throne is not merely a symbol of power but of right power — authority exercised in conformity with moral truth.
translator_notes: The combination of mishpat ('justice, judgment') and din ('cause, legal case') covers both the process and the outcome of divine adjudication. God has both heard the case and rendered the verdict. The dual legal terminology emphasizes thoroughness — no aspect of the psalmist's cause has been overlooked.

verses.text_hebrew: גָּעַ֣רְתָּ ג֭וֹיִם אִבַּ֣דְתָּ רָשָׁ֑ע שְׁמָ֥ם מָ֝חִ֗יתָ לְעוֹלָ֥ם וָעֶֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou hast rebuked the heathen, thou hast destroyed the wicked, thou hast put out their name for ever and ever.
verses.expanded_rendering: Three perfect-tense verbs declare completed divine action. Ga'arta goyim ('You have rebuked the nations') — ga'ar means to rebuke with such force that the object is silenced and driven back; it is used of God rebuking the sea (Psalm 104:7) and rebuking the Adversary (Zechariah 3:2). Ibbadta rasha ('You have destroyed the wicked') — the singular rasha may represent a category: wickedness personified. Shemam machita le-olam va-ed ('You have blotted out their name forever and ever') — to erase a name is the ultimate punishment in the ancient world, where survival after death depended on being remembered. Le-olam va-ed ('forever and ever, to perpetuity') makes the erasure permanent. No memory remains.
translator_notes: Name-erasure (blotting out the shem) is the opposite of the psalm's opening, where God's name is praised. The wicked's name disappears forever; God's name endures in praise. The contrast between permanent divine fame and permanent wicked oblivion is the psalm's core theological architecture.

verses.text_hebrew: הָאוֹיֵ֗ב תַּ֣מּוּ חֳרָב֣וֹת לָנֶ֑צַח וְעָרִ֥ים נָ֝תַ֗שְׁתָּ אָבַ֥ד זִכְרָֽם הֵֽמָּה׃
verses.text_kjv: O thou enemy, destructions are come to a perpetual end: and thou hast destroyed cities; their memorial is perished with them.
verses.expanded_rendering: The destruction of the enemy is described in three dimensions. Ha-oyev tammu choravot la-netsach ('the enemy — desolations have come to an end forever') — the enemy's power to create ruins (choravot) has itself become ruined. The destroyer is destroyed. Ve-arim natashta ('and cities You have uprooted') — the verb natash ('to pull up, to uproot, to tear out') applies to cities what is normally said of plants: God has pulled them out by the roots. Avad zikhram hemmah ('their memory has perished — they themselves') — even the memory (zekher) of the enemy cities is gone. The triple destruction — power ended, cities uprooted, memory erased — leaves nothing. The enemy has been removed from history itself.
translator_notes: The completeness of destruction — physical (cities), temporal (forever), and memorial (memory erased) — reflects the ancient Near Eastern understanding that true destruction means being forgotten. A ruined city that is still remembered can be rebuilt. A city whose memory is erased cannot even be mourned. This is annihilation at the deepest level.

verses.text_hebrew: וַֽ֭יהוָה לְעוֹלָ֣ם יֵשֵׁ֑ב כּוֹנֵ֖ן לַמִּשְׁפָּ֣ט כִּסְאֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: But the LORD shall endure for ever: he hath prepared his throne for judgment.
verses.rendering:
But the LORD sits enthroned forever;
He has established His throne for judgment.
verses.expanded_rendering: Against the transience of the wicked, God's permanence. Va-YHWH le-olam yeshev ('but the LORD sits forever') — the verb yashav ('to sit, to dwell, to remain') combined with le-olam ('forever') presents God as permanently enthroned. While enemies are uprooted and forgotten, God's seat remains. Konen la-mishpat kis'o ('He has established His throne for judgment') — the verb kun ('to establish, to make firm') means the throne is not temporary but permanent infrastructure. Its purpose: mishpat ('judgment, justice'). God's throne exists for the purpose of rendering justice. The permanence of God's rule guarantees the permanence of justice — even when justice seems delayed, the throne from which it will come is immovable.
translator_notes: The contrast between the enemy's memory perishing (v. 7) and the LORD sitting forever (v. 8) is the psalm's central theological claim: evil is temporary; God's justice is eternal. The throne imagery anticipates the great enthronement psalms (93, 95-99) that declare YHWH malakh ('the LORD reigns').

verses.text_hebrew: וְה֗וּא יִשְׁפֹּט־תֵּבֵ֥ל בְּצֶ֑דֶק יָדִ֥ין לְ֝אֻמִּ֗ים בְּמֵישָׁרִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: And he shall judge the world in righteousness, he shall minister judgment to the people in uprightness.
verses.rendering:
He judges the world with righteousness;
He governs the peoples with equity.
verses.expanded_rendering: God's judgment is characterized by two qualities. Yishpot tevel be-tsedeq ('He judges the world with righteousness') — tevel ('the world, the inhabited earth') is the full scope of God's jurisdiction. Tsedeq ('righteousness') is the standard. Yadin le'ummim be-mesharim ('He governs the peoples with equity, with uprightness') — mesharim (plural of mishor, 'level place, plain, equity') means evenness, fairness, straight dealing. God's judgment does not tilt toward the powerful or against the weak — it is level, like a balanced scale. The combination of tsedeq and mesharim establishes God as the judge who is both morally right (tsedeq) and procedurally fair (mesharim).
translator_notes: This verse will be repeated almost verbatim in Psalm 96:13 and Psalm 98:9, where it forms the climax of enthronement hymns. The language of cosmic, equitable divine judgment is one of the Psalter's most consistent theological claims — it transcends individual lament to make a universal statement about God's governance.

verses.text_hebrew: וִיהִ֣י יְ֭הוָה מִשְׂגָּ֣ב לַדָּ֑ךְ מִ֝שְׂגָּ֗ב לְעִתּ֥וֹת בַּצָּרָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD also will be a refuge for the oppressed, a refuge in times of trouble.
verses.rendering:
The LORD is a stronghold for the crushed,
a stronghold in times of distress.
verses.expanded_rendering: One of the psalm's most theologically significant declarations. Vihi YHWH misgav la-dakh ('the LORD is a stronghold for the crushed, the oppressed'). The word misgav ('stronghold, high refuge, inaccessible place') describes a fortress on a height — a place of safety that cannot be reached by attackers. The dakh ('crushed, oppressed, beaten down') is the person who has been ground down by power, circumstance, or injustice. God does not merely notice the crushed — He becomes their fortress. The parallel misgav le-ittot ba-tsarah ('a stronghold for times of distress') extends the promise beyond specific persons to specific moments: whenever crisis comes, God is the high place of safety.
translator_notes: The word dakh ('crushed') appears in Psalm 10:18 and describes the most vulnerable members of society — those who have been pressed down by forces they cannot resist. The Psalter's consistent claim is that God's power is directed toward these people specifically. This is not random benevolence but targeted justice: God fortifies the powerless.

verses.text_hebrew: וְיִבְטְח֣וּ בְ֭ךָ יוֹדְעֵ֣י שְׁמֶ֑ךָ כִּ֤י לֹֽא־עָזַ֖בְתָּ דֹרְשֶׁ֣יךָ יְהוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: And they that know thy name will put their trust in thee: for thou, LORD, hast not forsaken them that seek thee.
verses.rendering:
Those who know Your name trust in You,
for You have not abandoned those who seek You, O LORD.
verses.expanded_rendering: Trust is grounded in knowledge: ve-yivtechu vekha yod'e shemekha ('those who know Your name trust in You'). The verb yada ('to know') here means experiential knowledge — knowing God's character through encounter, not merely knowing facts about God. God's shem ('name') is His revealed character, and those who know it discover it to be trustworthy. The reason: ki lo azavta dorshekha YHWH ('for You have not abandoned those who seek You, O LORD'). The verb azav ('to abandon, to forsake, to leave behind') is negated with lo — God has not and does not forsake. The dorshim ('seekers,' from darash, 'to seek, to inquire, to investigate') are those who actively pursue God. The psalm's logic is: seek God, discover His character, and that discovery becomes the foundation of unshakeable trust.
translator_notes: The connection between knowing God's name and trusting God is a central Psalter theme. Trust (bitachon) is not blind — it is built on accumulated experience of God's faithfulness. The more one knows the name (the character), the more one trusts. This is relational knowledge, the kind that grows through time spent together.

key_terms.semantic_range: faithfulness, firmness, steadiness, reliability, truth; the quality of being dependable
key_terms.note: Though emunah does not appear in this verse directly, the concept undergirds the declaration that God 'has not abandoned those who seek Him.' emunah derives from aman ('to be firm, to be established, to confirm'). God's faithfulness is the objective reality that makes human trust rational. The Psalter's entire theology of trust rests on God's emunah — His proven, consistent, steadfast reliability.

verses.text_hebrew: זַמְּר֗וּ לַ֭יהוָה יֹשֵׁ֣ב צִיּ֑וֹן הַגִּ֥ידוּ בָ֝עַמִּ֗ים עֲלִילוֹתָֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: Sing praises to the LORD, which dwelleth in Zion: declare among the people his doings.
verses.rendering:
Sing praises to the LORD who dwells in Zion;
declare His deeds among the peoples.
translator_notes: The call to sing praises (zamm'ru) and declare (haggidu) God's deeds among the peoples (ba-ammim) extends worship beyond Israel to the nations. The LORD dwells in Zion (yoshev Tsiyyon) but His deeds concern all peoples. This outward-facing praise anticipates the missionary theology of Psalms 96, 98, and 117, where all nations are called to worship the LORD.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־דֹרֵ֣שׁ דָּ֭מִים אוֹתָ֣ם זָכָ֑ר לֹֽא־שָׁ֝כַ֗ח צַעֲקַ֥ת עֲנָוִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: When he maketh inquisition for blood, he remembereth them: he forgetteth not the cry of the humble.
verses.rendering:
For He who avenges blood remembers them;
He does not forget the cry of the afflicted.
verses.expanded_rendering: The theological foundation of the psalm's hope. Ki doresh damim otam zakhar ('for the one who seeks out blood — He remembers them'). The phrase doresh damim ('seeker of blood, avenger of blood') describes God as the one who investigates bloodshed and holds perpetrators accountable (cf. Genesis 9:5, 'I will require your blood'). Otam zakhar ('He remembers them') — the 'them' refers to the victims, the ones whose blood was shed. God does not forget the dead. Lo shakhach tsa'aqat anavim ('He does not forget the cry of the afflicted, the humble') — shakhach ('to forget') is negated: God's memory is permanent. The tsa'aqah ('cry, outcry, shriek') of the anavim ('afflicted, humble, lowly') reaches and remains in God's attention. The afflicted may feel forgotten, but the psalm insists they are not.
translator_notes: The anavim ('the afflicted, the humble, the lowly') are one of the Psalter's most important social categories. They are the poor, the oppressed, the powerless who depend entirely on God because human systems have failed them. The Psalter consistently claims that God hears specifically this group — not because they are morally superior but because they are vulnerable and have no other advocate.

verses.text_hebrew: חׇנְנֵ֬נִי יְהוָ֗ה רְאֵ֣ה עׇ֭נְיִי מִשֹּׂנְאָ֑י מְ֝רוֹמְמִ֗י מִשַּׁ֥עֲרֵי מָֽוֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: Have mercy upon me, O LORD; consider my trouble which I suffer of them that hate me, thou that liftest me up from the gates of death:
translator_notes: The phrase sha'are mavet ('gates of death') pictures death as a walled city with gates — once entered, there is no return. The psalmist testifies that God has lifted him (merom'mi) from those very gates — pulled him back from the threshold of death. The imagery recurs in Psalm 107:18 and Isaiah 38:10. The request to 'see my affliction' (re'eh onyi) appeals to God's visual attention — to look and therefore to act.

verses.text_hebrew: לְמַ֗עַן אֲסַפְּרָ֥ה כׇּל־תְּהִלָּתֶ֑יךָ בְּשַׁעֲרֵ֥י בַת־צִ֝יּ֗וֹן אָגִ֥ילָה בִּישׁוּעָתֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: That I may shew forth all thy praise in the gates of the daughter of Zion: I will rejoice in thy salvation.
verses.rendering:
so that I may declare all Your praises
in the gates of Daughter Zion,
that I may rejoice in Your salvation.
verses.expanded_rendering: Rescue has a purpose: le-ma'an asapperah kol tehillatekha ('so that I may recount all Your praises'). The psalmist is saved not merely for his own benefit but for testimony — to tell others what God has done. The location is be-sha'are vat Tsiyyon ('in the gates of Daughter Zion') — the gates are where public life happens, where courts convene, where news is shared. Daughter Zion is Jerusalem personified as a young woman. The contrast is vivid: pulled from the sha'are mavet ('gates of death,' v. 14), the psalmist now praises in the sha'are vat Tsiyyon ('gates of Daughter Zion,' v. 15). Death's gates threatened to close; Zion's gates open for praise. Agilah bi-yeshu'atekha ('I will rejoice in Your salvation') — the joy is specifically in God's saving act, not in general optimism.
translator_notes: The parallel between 'gates of death' (v. 14) and 'gates of Daughter Zion' (v. 15) is deliberately constructed. The two sets of gates represent the two possible destinations: death or life, silence or praise. God pulls the psalmist from one set of gates and sets him praising in the other.

verses.text_hebrew: טָבְע֣וּ ג֭וֹיִם בְּשַׁ֣חַת עָשׂ֑וּ בְּרֶ֥שֶׁת ז֝֗וּ טָמָ֥נוּ נִלְכְּדָ֥ה רַגְלָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: The heathen are sunk down in the pit that they made: in the net which they hid is their own foot taken.
verses.rendering:
The nations have sunk into the pit they made;
their own foot is caught in the net they hid.
verses.expanded_rendering: The self-defeating nature of evil, already celebrated in Psalm 7:16, returns. Tav'u goyim be-shachat asu ('the nations have sunk into the pit they made') — the verb tava ('to sink, to sink down, to be submerged') describes going under, drowning in one's own trap. The shachat ('pit, trap, snare') is the one they dug for others. Be-reshet zu tamanu nilk'dah raglam ('in the net that they hid, their own foot is caught') — the reshet ('net') was concealed as a trap, but the foot (regel) caught in it belongs to the one who set it. The justice is exact: the instrument of intended harm becomes the instrument of self-harm. No external punishment is needed — evil carries its own sentence.
translator_notes: The pit-and-net imagery draws on two hunting methods: the concealed pit (for large game) and the hidden net (for birds or smaller animals). Both depend on the victim not seeing the trap. The irony is that the trappers become their own victims — they know exactly where the trap is and yet somehow fall into it. This is the psalm's commentary on the blindness of wickedness.

verses.text_hebrew: נוֹדַ֤ע ׀ יְהוָ֗ה מִשְׁפָּ֣ט עָ֭שָׂה בְּפֹ֣עַל כַּפָּ֑יו נוֹקֵ֥שׁ רָ֝שָׁ֗ע הִגָּי֥וֹן סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD is known by the judgment which he executeth: the wicked is snared in the work of his own hands. Higgaion. Selah.
verses.rendering:
The LORD has made Himself known — He has executed judgment.
The wicked is snared by the work of his own hands.
Higgaion. Selah.
verses.expanded_rendering: A summary theological declaration. Noda YHWH mishpat asah ('the LORD has made Himself known; He has executed judgment') — God reveals His character through acts of justice. He is not known primarily through abstract theology but through visible intervention. Be-fo'al kappav noqesh rasha ('by the work of his own hands the wicked is snared') — the wicked person's own handiwork (fo'al kappav) becomes the trap (noqesh, from yaqash, 'to snare, to trap'). The term higgayon (from hagah, 'to meditate') may indicate a musical meditation or a pause for reflection. Combined with selah, it creates a double pause — the reader or singer is invited to stop and absorb the justice just described.
translator_notes: Higgayon appears only here and in Psalm 92:4 (where it describes the sound of a harp) and possibly Psalm 19:15 (where it means 'meditation'). Its placement here after the statement about divine justice suggests a contemplative pause — a moment to meditate on the reality that God's justice is self-executing through the wicked's own actions.

verses.text_hebrew: יָשׁ֣וּבוּ רְשָׁעִ֣ים לִשְׁא֑וֹלָה כׇּל־גּ֝וֹיִ֗ם שְׁכֵחֵ֥י אֱלֹהִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: The wicked shall be turned into hell, and all the nations that forget God.
verses.rendering:
The wicked will return to Sheol —
all the nations that forget God.
verses.expanded_rendering: A stark declaration of destiny. Yashuvu resha'im li-She'olah ('the wicked will return to Sheol') — the verb shuv ('to return') is significant: they will go back to Sheol, as if Sheol is their origin or natural destination. Sheol is the underworld, the place of the dead, the realm of silence and forgetting. Kol goyim shekheche Elohim ('all the nations that forget God') — the defining sin is forgetting. Shakhach ('to forget') is the opposite of zakhar ('to remember'). In verse 13, God does not forget the cry of the afflicted; here, nations that forget God face Sheol. The symmetry is devastating: those who forget God are forgotten by life itself.
translator_notes: The verb shuv ('to return') implies that Sheol is where the wicked belong — it is their natural habitat. They may temporarily walk among the living, but their destination was always the underworld. The nations' crime is not specified violence but forgetting — allowing God to drop from awareness, living as if God does not exist or does not matter.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י לֹ֣א לָ֭נֶצַח יִשָּׁכַ֣ח אֶבְי֑וֹן תִּקְוַ֥ת עֲ֝נָוִ֗ים תֹּאבַ֥ד לָעַֽד׃
verses.text_kjv: For the needy shall not alway be forgotten: the expectation of the poor shall not perish for ever.
verses.rendering:
For the needy will not always be forgotten;
the hope of the afflicted will not perish forever.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's most direct promise to the vulnerable. Ki lo la-netsach yishakhach evyon ('for not forever will the needy be forgotten') — the evyon ('needy, destitute, desperately poor') may feel forgotten now, but that forgetting has an expiration date. La-netsach ('forever, in perpetuity') is negated: the present condition is not the permanent condition. Tiqvat anavim tovad la-ad ('the hope of the afflicted will not perish forever') — tiqvah ('hope, expectation, cord of anticipation') is what the poor hold onto when everything else is gone. The psalm declares that this fragile hope will not be cut off (tovad, 'perish, be destroyed'). The double negative (not forever forgotten, hope will not perish) is itself a form of hope — expressed through the denial of despair rather than through triumphant declaration.
translator_notes: The pairing of evyon ('needy') and anavim ('afflicted') represents the most vulnerable members of society. The Psalter's consistent advocacy for these groups reflects the prophetic tradition (Amos 2:6-7, Isaiah 10:1-2) and anticipates the Beatitudes (Matthew 5:3-5). The promise here is not that suffering will end immediately but that it will not last forever — a distinction that matters enormously to those enduring it.

verses.text_hebrew: קוּמָ֣ה יְ֭הוָה אַל־יָעֹ֣ז אֱנ֑וֹשׁ יִשָּׁפְט֥וּ ג֝וֹיִ֗ם עַל־פָּנֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Arise, O LORD; let not man prevail: let the heathen be judged in thy sight.
verses.rendering:
Rise up, O LORD! Do not let mortals prevail;
let the nations be judged before You.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm returns to petition with the ancient battle cry: qumah YHWH ('rise up, O LORD'). The request: al ya'oz enosh ('do not let a mortal prevail, do not let a human being be strong'). The word enosh ('mortal, frail human') emphasizes human weakness — the same word from Psalm 8:5 that asked 'what is enosh?' The psalmist asks that frail human power not be allowed to triumph over divine justice. Yishaphtu goyim al panekha ('let the nations be judged before Your face') — God's face is the courtroom: to be judged before God's face is to face the undeflectable gaze of absolute justice.
translator_notes: The word enosh ('mortal') is deliberately chosen over adam or ish to emphasize human frailty. The prayer is that fragile human power — which often seems overwhelming when wielded against the vulnerable — not be allowed to overrule divine justice. The nations may be powerful, but before God's face they are merely enosh: mortal, weak, temporary.

verses.text_hebrew: שִׁ֘יתָ֤ה יְהוָ֨ה ׀ מוֹרָ֗ה לָהֶ֥ם יֵדְע֥וּ גוֹיִ֑ם אֱ֝נ֗וֹשׁ הֵ֣מָּה סֶּֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Put them in fear, O LORD: that the nations may know themselves to be but men. Selah.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's final petition is for a revelation of human limitation. Shitah YHWH morah lahem ('place terror upon them, O LORD') — morah ('fear, terror, dread') is to be placed on the nations as a divine imposition. The purpose: yed'u goyim enosh hemmah ('let the nations know that they are enosh, that they are merely mortal'). The prayer is not for destruction but for self-knowledge — let the nations discover what they truly are: enosh, mortal, frail, temporary. The psalm that marveled at human dignity in Psalm 8 ('what is enosh?') now prays that the powerful will recognize the limits of that dignity. To know you are enosh is the beginning of wisdom; to forget it is the beginning of tyranny. Selah closes the psalm, inviting meditation on the distance between human pretension and human reality.
translator_notes: The prayer for nations to know they are 'only human' (enosh hemmah) is one of the Psalter's most penetrating petitions. It does not ask for the nations' destruction but for their enlightenment — specifically, the enlightenment of recognizing their own mortality and limitation. The most dangerous people are those who forget they are enosh. The psalm asks God to remind them.
